claimed_contribution: The paper introduces a process-transfer model of endorser effects in which the evaluation process activated by judging an endorser carries over to judging the endorsed product.

The authors claim this model supplements affect-transfer, meaning-transfer, and source-credibility models, and helps explain successful endorsements by less favorable or less credible endorsers and under low endorser-product congruence.

The paper claims that subtle verbal and visual cues in ads can induce process transfer, offering less restrictive endorser selection and a practical damage-control tool when endorsers develop negative publicity.
context: The paper studies celebrity endorsement advertising across multiple product and service settings, including Pepsi, sparkling wine, fish oil, camcorders, and a British restaurant. It focuses on real celebrities with negative public images (Edison Chen, Paris Hilton, Kim Kardashian, and Jaycee Chan), motivated by the Rich Prosecco-Paris Hilton case.
design_and_data: The authors run five studies: one field experiment in Hong Kong with real purchase choices (N = 184) and four controlled experiments (MTurk N = 446, 306, and 618; lab N = 358). Across studies, they manipulate ad cues to induce a who-one-is versus what-one-does evaluation process, using either verbal descriptions or subtle visual cues tied to endorser origin versus achievements. They compare responses to paired product versions that are stronger on origin-related attributes (for example COO or brand background) versus performance-related attributes (for example taste, nutrition, resolution, or food quality), with controls such as no-process/no-endorser conditions in Experiment 2. Outcomes include actual choice and purchase or visit intention, with mediation measures (attribute-importance weighting and analogous-process use), plus checks for endorser negativity, product positioning salience, involvement, and perceived fit.
main_findings: Inducing a who-one-is process increased preference for origin-dominant options, while inducing a what-one-does process increased preference for performance-dominant options, including in a field purchase setting (69.47% vs 48.31% choosing COO-dominant Pepsi).

The effects were mediated by process carryover: participants weighted COO versus performance attributes differently and reported greater use of conceptually analogous criteria when evaluating endorser and product.

When product positioning cues were made salient, positioning could override endorser-induced process transfer; and the effect weakened under extremely low involvement but held under moderate to high involvement, even with low endorser-product fit.
one_line_summary: Across a field study and four experiments, the paper shows that ad cues that trigger who-one-is versus what-one-does evaluation of a celebrity transfer to product evaluation and shift preference toward origin- versus performance-dominant offerings, even with negatively viewed endorsers.
relation_to_prior_work: extends existing affect-transfer, meaning-transfer, and source-credibility accounts by proposing a valence-neutral mechanism in which evaluation procedures, not just valenced properties, transfer from endorser to product. It also challenges strict fit-based constraints by showing process transfer can operate across unrelated endorser-product domains, while identifying conditions where other cues dominate.
research_gap: Prior endorser-effect models emphasize transfer of positive valence (likeability, credibility, symbolic meanings) and typically predict weaker outcomes for disliked, less credible, or low-fit endorsers. The paper argues these models do not explain some successful endorsements in such cases and do not consider transfer of the evaluation process itself.
research_question: Does the cognitive process consumers use to evaluate an endorser transfer to evaluation of the endorsed product? Specifically, can inducing a who-one-is versus what-one-does mindset through ad cues shift product judgments toward origin-related versus performance-related attributes, including when endorsers are negatively viewed or low in fit with the product? The paper also examines when this process-transfer effect is strengthened or attenuated.
